speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape number two, and we are interviewing Ambassador Per Anger. So you tell me about this Nobel Prize man that you saved, and that was the first person that the Swedish Legation saved. What happened to him?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well then, of course, I rushed back to the embassy. And we were evacuating from there, because you never knew. You never knew if the-- what the Germans were going to-- to do if-- if they knew that we were hiding him.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we took him away from there. And we were hiding him then on different places in the-- in the city. And to make a long story short, just-- he was living with me, towards the end.

speaker: interviewee
text: We'll just say in-- in January, beginning of January, 1945, in-- I had a small-- small room I had up in the-- near the-- the palace, up in the middle of-- of Buda.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it the same house that you were renting, the part of the house you were renting from the beginning?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no. We-- I had to move from there, already. And so I was hiding him in there. And then-- and then I told his son-in-law, which was-- became a very good friend of mine, Gyorgy Libik, who was in the resistance. And he-- I told-- I said to him, now, you take my car now. And you go over to Pest side, because I had the feeling that the Soviets will come very soon. And that he did. And after he had went over, with his father-in-law, the Germans blew up all the bridges. The-- the Soviets came into the Pest side, attacked the Pest side and all that. And he came into contact with the Soviets, this Professor Szent-Gyorgy. And they brought him to Moscow, where he was-- they paid tribute to him there as the big man. And-- and that then he was going to be the head of state and all that and so on. Well, he-- he became so fed up with the Communists' sort of situation, so he used-- he took the first opportunity then, later on, to-- to escape from Hungary. He was lecturing then in different countries. And-- and one day, he just stayed on. So he stayed on in-- in the United States for all the years then after the war. And-- and he founded a-- a kind of-- he had a laboratory there. And he was working in research. And he-- he was the man who, more or less, found out about the vitamin C. That was his invention, so to say. That was why he became-- actually, why he got the Nobel Prize.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have the feeling that Germans knew that you were hiding him?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well they--

speaker: interviewer
text: At the first time, when-- when he was hiding in the Swedish Delegation?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. Well, they-- they-- they knew about it. But they were, of course-- they-- they gave-- Bruenhoff gave me that warning, you see? And that, I'm very grateful for-- for that. And so that's how it works when you have good friends, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: Taking about Raoul Wallenberg, were you in Budapest when he arrived?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I was there in-- in July, on the 9th of July when he came. And he had been traveling then nonstop from Stockholm. He came very late. He-- many people say, why did he come that late? Well, you see, that was a very complicated situation, because we-- we-- till we-- I mean, you were facing a-- a new situation in Budapest when the Germans had arrived. When I went back to-- when I-- That morning after the occupation, when I walked back-- walked to-- to the office in the morning, and met every third-- sort of, third person with a yellow star on, I was-- it was a shock, of course, a great shock for me. It-- it was a disgrace, a dishonor that-- that in modern times, you-- you'd disqualify a whole race like that, that they are not worthy of being a member of society and along and all that. And all these regulations, that the Jews, the Jewish people were not allowed to sit on the-- on the-- on the chairs and everything in the-- in the parks. They were not allowed to travel by train. They were not allowed. They would had to live in-- in certain houses. All this just came like-- upon us, like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was it-- was it the first day they had the yellow star on them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, more or less. They-- they-- they operated quickly, like that, you see, the Germans. And then-- then the question was, what can we do now?

speaker: interviewer
text: They were queuing up, outside our embassy and outside the Swiss and outside the other neutral embassies, pleading for help. And that-- what could we do? Well-- very hard.

speaker: interviewee
text: There was nothing in our book of instructions, who told us what-- how we could save people of other nationalities. We-- we are representing Sweden. We are-- are helping Swedish citizens and all that, with the working for the relationship between Sweden and that country where we are staying and all that.

speaker: interviewer
text: But to take a whole group of-- of other nationals under our protection, and-- and how could we do that? But that's the-- the time when I came to think of, the only way we can do this is to give-- and we got the authorisation from Sweden--

speaker: interviewee
text: They have-- there were so many relatives, Jewish relatives in Stockholm and all that, who were pressing the-- pushing the Foreign Office to do something and all that. So we suggested that those who have relations with Sweden, relatives or commercial relations, we could give them a visa to Sweden.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, visa? They couldn't travel of course. They couldn't travel through Germany. So what could you do? Well, I invented then a kind of a certificate, where I said that the holder of this paper has got a visa to Sweden. Until he would be able to travel to Sweden, he would be under the protection of the Swedish government, with stamps and things on that they had to see, a completely legal document.

speaker: interviewee
text: But the-- the Nazis, especially the Hungarian Nazis, who already, at that time, had come up to surface like that, there were many of them illiterate. And they were impressed by stamps and sort of things.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we saved a couple of hundred people in doing this and in issuing these papers. But we couldn't cope with the-- the ever increasing number of applicants. So we sent for help from-- from Sweden. At the-- at the beginning, these first 200 person, was it any known person during--

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Well--

speaker: interviewer
text: --with that-- with that group?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, certainly there were. There were-- they were-- they were a-- a strong group of business people, I remember. And-- and a step before that was when I took on my own to give them Swedish passports.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's what we call a Swedish provisional passport. That's in, in a way, only a traveling-- traveling document. When the-- when a Swede comes up to the embassy and says, look here, I've lost my passport. Well, can you prove that? No. Well, here is a-- a document, a provisional passport. We give to you, and you go to Sweden and prove your identity.

speaker: interviewee
text: That kind of a document, of course, was not-- were never give-- given to any foreigners. But I did it. I took the risk. And-- and the Swedish government approved, afterwards, this action and so on. And it's interesting to know that this first group, I gave those passports to, were the-- the big business people. For instance, there was the-- the head of the Tungsram. [INAUDIBLE] And that's the big factory, working lamps and all that, electrical firm. And that was a Swedish-owned. And I remember, still remember when he came to me. His name was Wohl, Hugo Wohl. And he come up to me in my apartment, hiding his yellow star, like that, and said, Per, now you must help me.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I said, what-- what can I do? I mean it-- it's-- And then I came to think of, why don't I give you a pass, a Swedish passport, like that, a Swedish provisional passport, he and his family, and-- and-- also his next in command and so on and so on.

speaker: interviewee
text: And those-- this group of people then, later on, became the-- the closest cooperators-- collaborators with Raoul Wallenberg. And you can imagine the-- the competence. I mean, they have been heading big firms and organizers and used to-- to solve situations in different-- different aspects like that. If you already have-- have been the boss for hundreds of people working in a factory and all that and so on, you had a routine and to go on organizing and-- and how to put the right man in the right place and so on.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they became the staff, this-- the first staff of Raoul Wallenberg. And they put up all these-- of his offices. And so Raoul Wallenberg had very, very competent co-workers at the beginning. Because I had already saved them before he came, so to say.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then-- then when Raoul Wallenberg comes, then-- then he, with his ingenuity and inventiveness and all that, he-- when he saw these-- these certificates I had issued, he said, well, this is fine. But perhaps we could make them more spectacular. And that is how this Schutz-Pass, protective passports came about in Swedish colors and the Swedish coat of arms on and all that and so on. And then how he negotiated then with the then Hungarian government and got the approval of 4,500 passports. And the-- the holders of this passport could then be hidden or lodged in houses, Swedish houses or so-called Swedish houses, which we rented or bought, with the help of American money. Because there, it was the American initiative, all this mission. And the Americans were financing Raoul Wallenberg's action behind the scene.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you remember the first time you met Raoul Wallenberg?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you remember the first time you met Raoul Wallenberg?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well-- well, of course, I had met him long before. I met him in Sweden. And we were students together. I met him when he came to Budapest as a businessman. He was working in a business firm in Stockholm, so he came there in '42, '43, already, long before all this happened.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I knew him very well. So when I now met him for this mission, I-- I knew that he was-- was the right man for the job. Did you know the purpose of his mission, when he came?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, certainly. We asked-- we asked for-- for-- for some more staff. And the-- the first idea was to send Count Bernadotte, Folke Bernadotte, with the Red Cross delegation to Budapest. But Bernadotte never got the visa, the permission, by the Hungarians or the Germans, to-- to-- to go there. So then, it so happened that Raoul Wallenberg volunteered. And he was and sent-- sent there.

speaker: interviewer
text: During that time, did you were occupied with your trade work, with your commercial work?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh no, that-- that-- that was not-- [LAUGHS] It-- it, of course, was interesting in a way. But there were more important tasks now for me, and that-- that was to save peoples' lives. So that was my job, to join the Raoul Wallenberg sort of mission in a way. You see, this is interesting to see how a small embassy-- we were five people, my boss, myself, a chancellery officer, a driver, two girls, that was the whole. And-- and we asked for more staff. Raoul Wallenberg came. We got some other helpers, also. --how that grew into an-- an enormous organization, with me sitting in the center to try to coordinate all this. That was the Raoul Wallenberg very, very important mission, action, with all these offices and all that. We had the Swedish Red Cross, who was working along the same lines as Raoul Wallenberg but on a much smaller scale. We had a Save the Children, a-- a-- a-- a rather old lady at the time, who came also to our help and were organizing a children's home for Jewish children. You had a big section for all the interests we had under the countries. You know, during the war, at the war-- at the wartime, the neutrals, like during the First World War and after-- and also during the Second World War, Switzerland and Sweden more or less divided the world in two, so to say.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we had in Budapest-- the-- the Swiss had the British, the Americans, and all these interests. You can't keep your embassies, as you understand, in your enemy city. So we took over other embassies.

speaker: interviewee
text: We took over the Dutch and the Finnish and the-- many of these. We had a big section for those, what we called, safeguarding of the other countries' interests, their citizens, who were, perhaps, left behind, their buildings and so on.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that was a big job in the Swedish Foreign Office during the war. We had a big department in Stockholm heading this. And at-- at every embassy in-- where the-- the country was in war, had a section to do-- to deal with those questions. Could you please describe a very ordinary day of your life during that time? Do you went to the office in the morning and what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, of course, I did. And-- and I tried then to-- to-- to help Raoul Wallenberg. There was the-- of course, how could you supply these houses with food and all that? That was also a big organization, too. We sent people out in the countryside to buy on the black market, to buy-- to buy-- to buy food, also with the help of American money, to supply and medicine and all that. Imagine, they-- they lived in these houses to start with 5,000 people and perhaps then we, in all secrecy, we increased that. So towards the end of the war, when we assessed this situation, we found that at least around 20,000 people were living there, and with the Swedish protective passports, and were all saved, more or less.

speaker: interviewer
text: So my job was to give him, give Wallenberg some advice and so on. There was always discussions, between him and me, how far can we go? We have now illegally given not only 5,000 but perhaps, slowly, for 20,000 people passports and so on. How far can we go? We can certainly not give the whole population protective passports, because then it will lose its value. So it's a balance that whole time, which is very cruel that you-- you had to say, denied, to some. I'm sorry, we can't give you a passport. We had-- you had to prove that you have relatives in Sweden, you have that and that and that. So you had to, at least officially, be very restrictive. You had to build up a bureaucracy. That was our-- our weapon, in a way, against the Germans, to show them, look here, we-- we are doing everything legally here.

speaker: interviewee
text: We have these criteria. People who give-- get this passport have to have Swedish relationship and so on and so on. We have officers, here, all sitting and-- and scrutinizing all the backgrounds of all these people and all. Very thorough work, you know. And we can show you all the dossier and all that and so on.

speaker: interviewer
text: That was how it-- how it went, you see? Which didn't prevent, of course, Wallenberg, towards the very end, when everything was falling in pieces, to give away passports like that. That was the last weeks before the-- the Russians came. What kind of problem did you get with the Germans?

speaker: interviewee
text: What kind of a--

speaker: interviewer
text: Problem did you get with the Germans? Did--

speaker: interviewee
text: With the Germans? Well, we didn't have-- The problems were, of course, that-- that was the negotiations between Wallenberg and Eichmann. That when he tried to get Eichmann to understand, that he must now understand, that you are losing the war and all that, and that you would-- it would be to your advantage to show some-- give some liberty here to the Jews, not to-- to go on along the same lines as you-- You could perhaps save your own life. But he'd never listen. He never listened to that. So, he went on to the bitter end, you know, and so on. Now, he was, of course, restricted at the beginning. Because Horthy had according-- after the appeal from the Swedish King Gustaf V, had stopped all the-- all the deportations from Budapest.

speaker: interviewer
text: So first, Eichmann had no possibility to act. What he had did-- what he had done, already, of course, already starting in-- in March, was when he-- he evacuated the whole countryside, when he brought 600,000 Jewish people to Auschwitz. So when Wallenberg comes, it's-- it's a concentration then on our-- on our part, to try to save at least as many Jewish people in Budapest as possible. We had this favorable climate to start with, when Horthy had to stop the deportations. So during that time, Eichmann couldn't do very much. But then comes the new situation, after the revolution on the 15th of October, when Horthy tried to jump the ship. And the-- the Germans immediately reacted and deported him to Germany and gave all the power to the Hungarian-- to the Hungarian Nazis.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then comes this-- the situation with negotiations with Eichmann, trying to-- to stop him to go on then and all that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you meet Eichmann--

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: --at any time?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. At-- I-- that was left to Wallenberg. You see, we had this-- this sort of organization. We had a traditional Swedish embassy, with my boss, the ambassador, who was already, at that time, 65 years old. And he was going to retire. But he volunteered and stayed on. The other ambassadors, the other-- other embassies, they-- they just left. They-- they-- they didn't stay on. I mean, they were-- I-- I won't say they were a coward, but anyhow, they-- they-- they left. So the other embassies were-- were left in-- in-- in the hands of younger people and so on. But we had this great advantage to have a traditional embassy intact, with-- be the-- from the top till the-- to-- to-- to-- to the other staff working, where it gave us the status, at-- at least in the eyes of the-- of the Germans, and-- and-- and the status which the other embassies didn't have so much. But here is the traditional Swedish embassy working according to-- to the national law and all that. Then, of course, they understood that we had another member of the embassy, Wallenberg, who didn't act in a very conventional way. But we-- we let him do-- We-- we sort of closed our eyes. And we let him do that. Because we thought it was the only way. But it made a difference, in a way, that here is, we are working according to the old rules and all that. Raoul Wallenberg is, yes, he-- he has this special mission. And yes, he is authorized to-- to save as many people's lives as-- as-- as possible. So-- so that-- that made it-- that was, for me, also then very important not to go around and-- and meet and negotiate with Nazi people, here and there. I maintained the official relations with the German embassy and with the Hungarian Foreign Office. That was my role. It didn't prevent me from rush out and help Raoul on his-- on-- on-- when he was at the station, to help him, to liberate people and all that. That I did.

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewee
text: But I negotiated with other people in the foreign office and the-- that-- that made us being sort of considered as-- that gave us credibility-- credibility, you see, in the eyes of the-- of the Germans.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe one of these days when you went with Raoul Wallenberg to the train station to save people?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I-- I was with him several times. And I don't recall very much of all these different occasions. But I, of course, remember when he-- because I did it myself.

speaker: interviewer
text: Because he telephoned me one day and said, could you help me now, because I have to go to another station to save people. But there is one station where I know-- I got the report that there is a train just going to leave. Could you go there and try to do something? What kind of train was it-- was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, it was a cargo sort of cars.

speaker: interviewer
text: Cattlers?

speaker: interviewee
text: A cattle car. Cattle cars.

speaker: interviewer
text: Cattle cars.

speaker: interviewee
text: Cattle cars. So I went there. And-- and I said to myself, well, why don't I try to copy him, do it as he used to do it? So I said to the German commandant, open the doors, here, of this car, the cars. I'm a Swedish diplomat. And well, he was very hesitant. Well, if you don't do that, I have to report to your boss, to this-- to-- to the German embassy.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was he, a member of the SS or the Wehrmacht?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, he was an-- an SS man. Anyhow, I-- I have to report to the German embassy if you don't open the doors here. Because I suspect that there are-- there are people there who have Swedish passports. And you can't deport them.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he opened the doors. And I went in. And I said, are there any here? Is there anybody here who has a Swedish passport, a protective passport? And only two. And then I said to them, as I learned from-- from Raoul Wallenberg, but I recognize you, over there. You, you and the young lady opposite you, you want a passport? And-- and you there and so on. But I don't understand, you-- you must have some papers showing that you have got passports? And have you any certificate or anything about that? Were you speaking, talking German at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: In German. In-- in-- in German. And there they showed me different receipts or driving licenses or whatever they had on them. Oh yes, that was in the Hungarian language, which the Germans didn't understand. Oh, I see. Well that proves that you have got-- you have got a passport, all of you here. And come with me. And-- and I took out 100 people, and I put them into our houses.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were-- were you picking them in different wagon-- from different wagon?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, that was--

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewee
text: --they were packed. They were packed in-- in one big wagon, you see? You see, they were standing like that, you see?

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you pick up men or women and children, too?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes, all of them.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the reaction of the commandant?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the commander, he accepted it, because I acted as it was a kind of-- quite a legal thing that I-- I take the Jewish under our protection, that I take them with me.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you take them with you? Did you have a car? Did they-- did they walk?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. We was marching over there. That was quite near to the [NON-ENGLISH]. It was not far away from that station, I remember. So we marched away. And-- and-- and to tell you the truth, of course, I had to add one thing. That I had a great help of a Hungarian police officer, who was in our service, which, of course, the Germans didn't know. He was bribed in a way that I had promised him that, if he cooperates, I will see to it that he, after the war, will-- will become a resident of Switzerland or any country where-- when he want to leave this, which I-- which I did then, later on. He survived the whole thing. And they-- I don't think that they ever suspected that he was on our side and all. Anyhow.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Batis Falvi was his name, Batis Falvi. He was a-- a police officer and was standing there, in uniform, with all of the medals on-- on-- on. A very impressive person, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: So the Germans didn't suspect, other than, now, he's going to take them, perhaps to a German-- to an Hungarian prison. So that was the game we were playing, you know, with him, with his help. But what was happening physically with all these people that you saved? That particular day, these people, did they went to the safe house?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, yes. Oh yes, we took them to the safe houses. He more or less marched with them to there. And-- and there were Germans, who thought, oh, now, they're going-- he going to take them to prison.

speaker: interviewer
text: We have to stop for a while, because we are changing tape one more time.
